notes: Original balanced 200-question pack created after J! Archive difficulty calibration.
Designed to exercise the shared intake quality, feedback, and difficulty rules.

questions.clue: This author sent Alice down a rabbit hole and through a looking-glass.
This novelist wrote Beloved and Song of Solomon and won the Nobel Prize in Literature.
This Chilean author wrote The House of the Spirits and is the niece of a former president.
This Nigerian novelist wrote Things Fall Apart about Okonkwo and colonial disruption.
This Austen novel follows Elizabeth Bennet and Mr. Darcy through misunderstandings and proposals.
This Hugo novel centers on Jean Valjean after his release from prison.
This Shakespeare play strands Prospero and Miranda on an island with Ariel and Caliban.
This Sophocles tragedy features a king who seeks a plague's cause and discovers himself.
This poet addressed a Grecian urn and a nightingale in famous odes.
This medieval English poet framed stories as a pilgrimage to Canterbury.
This book by E. B. White features a spider who writes words in a web for a pig.
This Philip Pullman novel begins His Dark Materials with Lyra Belacqua and her daemon.
This Salinger novel follows Holden Caulfield after he leaves Pencey Prep.
This Steinbeck novel follows the Joad family from Oklahoma toward California.
This Virginia Woolf novel follows Clarissa through a London day as she prepares for a party.
This Argentine author wrote Labyrinths and stories about mirrors, libraries, and infinite books.
This orphan from Kansas travels with a scarecrow, tin man, and lion.
This Cervantes sidekick rides a donkey and serves a deluded knight.
This literary movement used interior monologue and fractured chronology in writers like Joyce and Woolf.
This annual British prize for fiction was first awarded in 1969 and went to P. H. Newby.
This president purchased Louisiana from France while Meriwether Lewis was his secretary.
This president took office after Lincoln's assassination and later faced impeachment.
This 1876 contest ended Reconstruction through a disputed compromise after the vote.
This president's administration negotiated the Gadsden Purchase from Mexico.
This 1862 act granted western settlers 160 acres after residence and improvement.
This 1919 constitutional change banned the manufacture and sale of alcoholic beverages.
This Harding-era affair involved naval oil reserves in Wyoming and California.
This Macedonian king created an empire stretching from Greece to Egypt and India.
This Roman emperor made Christianity legal with the Edict of Milan.
This plague devastated Europe in the mid-14th century.
This 1453 event ended the Byzantine Empire after an Ottoman siege.
This 1648 settlement helped end the Thirty Years' War and reshaped European sovereignty.
This Prussian minister engineered German unification through blood and iron.
This U.S. naval base was attacked on December 7, 1941.
This German plan for a two-front war called for a rapid attack through Belgium.
This prison's fall is celebrated as a French national holiday on July 14.
This Indian dynasty built the Taj Mahal and ruled from the 16th to 19th centuries.
This Haitian revolutionary led forces against France before dying in captivity in 1803.
This 1215 charter is often cited as a foundation for limits on royal power.
This 1949 alliance was founded for collective defense across the North Atlantic.
This capital of Greece lies near the Acropolis.
This capital of Egypt sits on the Nile near Giza.
This capital of Peru was founded by Francisco Pizarro and is nicknamed the City of Kings.
This capital of Ghana lies on the Gulf of Guinea.
This republic shares an island with the U.K. territory that includes Belfast.
This country borders Germany, Austria, Slovakia, and Poland and has Prague as capital.
This landlocked African country is surrounded by Senegal except for a short Atlantic coast.
This state capital sits on the James River and was also the Confederate capital.
This state contains Mount Katahdin and the easternmost point in the contiguous U.S.
This river runs through London before reaching the North Sea.
This South American river system includes the Parana and Uruguay rivers.
This Central Asian sea has shrunk dramatically after river diversions for irrigation.
This North African mountain range runs through Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia.
This Chilean region is among the driest places on Earth.
This New York City borough is connected to Brooklyn by the Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge.
This region of France is known for sparkling wine made around Reims and Epernay.
This white marble mausoleum in Agra was built by Shah Jahan.
This ancient Nabataean city in Jordan includes a rock-cut facade called Al-Khazneh.
This Celtic language is spoken in Wales and uses double-L sounds in many place names.
This imaginary line at zero degrees longitude passes through Greenwich.
This process copies DNA into RNA before translation can make a protein.
This organelle contains chlorophyll and performs photosynthesis in plant cells.
This enzyme unzips DNA during replication by breaking hydrogen bonds.
This element with symbol Na is a soft metal that reacts strongly with water.
This gas exhaled by humans is produced by respiration and combustion.
This chemical process transfers electrons between species and includes oxidation.
Named for James Prescott, this SI unit measures energy.
This law says pressure and volume of a gas vary inversely at constant temperature.
This observed shift changes wavelength when a source moves toward or away from an observer.
This planet has the Great Red Spot and the largest mass in the Solar System.
This moon of Jupiter has a subsurface ocean and an icy crust.
This boundary marks where two tectonic plates slide past each other horizontally.
This scale measures tornado intensity from EF0 to EF5.
This blood protein carries oxygen with the help of iron.
This pair of bean-shaped organs filters blood and produces urine.
This marine mammal uses echolocation and includes orcas among its largest members.
This scientist's 1928 observation of mold killing bacteria led to penicillin.
This primatologist studied chimpanzees at Gombe Stream in Tanzania.
This mathematician and physicist wrote A Brief History of Time while studying black holes.
This quantity relates photon energy to frequency in quantum physics.
This Mexican artist painted many self-portraits with symbolic animals and plants.
This French sculptor created The Thinker as part of a larger Gates of Hell project.
This American painter used drip techniques in works like Autumn Rhythm.
This painting by Edvard Munch shows a figure under a swirling orange sky.
This Picasso mural responded to the bombing of a Basque town during the Spanish Civil War.
This Diego Velazquez painting shows the Infanta Margarita surrounded by attendants.
This movement by Picasso and Braque broke subjects into geometric planes.
This movement associated with Marcel Duchamp embraced absurdity after World War I.
This Roman building with a giant dome includes an oculus open to the sky.
This architect designed the glass pyramid outside the Louvre.
This Smithsonian institution houses the Hope Diamond and many fossil displays.
This Madrid museum is known for a collection including Bosch, Velazquez, and Goya.
This Depression-era photograph by Dorothea Lange shows Florence Owens Thompson.
This photographer of Moonrise, Hernandez helped make Yosemite a black-and-white icon.
This German school founded by Walter Gropius joined art, craft, and architecture.
This style used flowing plant forms and helped shape posters by Alphonse Mucha.
This polished black wall in Washington lists names of U.S. service members lost in Southeast Asia.
This Chicago sculpture by Anish Kapoor reflects the skyline in Millennium Park.
This painting technique uses strong contrasts of light and dark for dramatic effect.
This critic championed abstract expressionism and the idea of flatness in modern painting.
This composer wrote The Four Seasons as a set of violin concertos.
This composer of The Rite of Spring caused a famous 1913 Paris uproar.
This Finnish composer wrote Finlandia and seven numbered symphonies.
This Puccini opera features the aria Nessun dorma.
This Verdi opera about an Ethiopian princess includes a triumphal march.
This musical about the first Treasury secretary uses hip-hop to tell early U.S. history.
This Sondheim show sends fairy-tale characters beyond happy endings after Act I.
This Kander and Ebb musical is set in a Berlin nightclub as Weimar Germany collapses.
This British band released Abbey Road and Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band.
This singer's album Purple Rain doubled as a film soundtrack.
This New Orleans trumpeter helped popularize jazz singing and improvisation.
This saxophonist recorded A Love Supreme with his classic quartet.
This large brass instrument often wraps around the player's body and plays low notes.
This double-reed woodwind tunes orchestras with an A before concerts.
This term names the speed of a piece of music.
This interval spans eight diatonic scale degrees from one note to its namesake higher note.
This Argentine dance is known for close embrace and sharp leg movements.
This Japanese theater form uses masks, slow movement, and a chorus.
This composer scored Jaws, Star Wars, E.T., and Jurassic Park.
This Italian composer scored The Good, the Bad and the Ugly and Cinema Paradiso.
This god ruled the underworld and carried off Persephone.
This craftsman built wings for himself and his son to escape Crete.
This Norse god loses a hand when the wolf Fenrir is bound.
This Norse hall receives warriors chosen by Odin's Valkyries.
This Egyptian god of the sun was often represented with a falcon head.
This Aztec feathered serpent deity has a name meaning precious serpent.
This man built an ark before a flood covered the earth.
This woman from Moab says where you go, I will go, to Naomi.
This minor prophet is associated with a valley of dry bones in a vision of restoration.
This faith's sacred river Ganges is central to pilgrimage and purification.
This religion founded in Punjab uses the Guru Granth Sahib as scripture.
This Sanskrit term for action and consequence is central in Indian religions.
This Jewish mystical tradition includes the Zohar as a central text.
This Chinese teacher is associated with Analects and filial piety.
This philosopher wrote The Second Sex and helped shape existential feminism.
This school associated with Pyrrho suspends judgment because certainty is difficult.
This school of Indian philosophy is classically dualist and paired with Yoga.
This Kantian command asks people to act only by principles they could will as universal law.
This Islamic month of fasting ends with Eid al-Fitr.
This Jewish Day of Atonement is marked by fasting and confession.
This adjective means able to use both hands skillfully.
This noun means a confused mixture or jumble, especially of ideas or words.
This adjective means stubbornly refusing to change an opinion.
This word for a fellow traveler or dining partner comes from Latin roots meaning with bread.
This word for a farewell comes from a contraction of God be with ye.
This homophone of flour names the colorful part of a plant.
This homophone of waist means to spend carelessly or use up.
Rearrange RESCUE to name a word meaning make safe or fasten.
Rearrange MASTER to name a small flowing body of water.
Rearrange INTEGRAL to name a three-sided geometric figure.
Wizard of Oz road + famous Beatles album.
Greek hero with a heel + Brad Pitt film set in ancient Troy.
Fitzgerald millionaire + Batman's home city.
In internet addresses, this three-letter abbreviation commonly begins a web URL.
This Latin abbreviation means and the rest.
This Spanish phrase literally means until the sight and is used as goodbye.
This German word names a spirit of the age.
This punctuation mark joins independent clauses more strongly than a comma.
In Rhyme Time, this phrase could mean a speedy baby bird.
In this idiom, revealing a secret is letting the cat out of this.
This team plays home games at Fenway Park.
In baseball, this three-letter statistic credits a batter when a plate appearance drives home a run.
This pitcher threw a perfect game in the 1956 World Series.
This NFL team plays home games at Lambeau Field.
This position usually snaps the ball to the quarterback.
This NBA team won six championships with Michael Jordan in the 1990s.
This coach led UCLA men's basketball to ten NCAA championships.
This international tournament awards a trophy every four years to a national team champion.
This Brazilian forward was born Edson Arantes do Nascimento.
This tennis tournament is played on grass at the All England Club.
This golfer was nicknamed the Golden Bear.
This city hosted the first modern Olympic Games in 1896.
This track athlete won four gold medals at the 1936 Berlin Olympics.
This baseball award honors each league's top pitcher.
This soccer award from France Football honors the world's top player.
In tennis scoring, this word means a tied score after both players reach forty.
In chess, this move protects the king by moving it two squares toward a rook.
This board game uses properties like Boardwalk, Park Place, and railroads.
This card game's name comes from a Spanish word for basket.
This Nintendo franchise centers on Samus Aran and alien parasites.
This 1982 Spielberg film follows a stranded alien who wants to phone home.
This 1975 shark thriller made Steven Spielberg a blockbuster director.
This Japanese director made Seven Samurai and Rashomon.
This sitcom featured Central Perk and characters named Rachel, Ross, Monica, Chandler, Joey, and Phoebe.
This HBO drama follows Tony Soprano and his New Jersey crime family.
This Google-owned video platform launched in 2005.
This term names a small image or video spread rapidly online with variations.
This Kryptonian superhero works at the Daily Planet when not wearing a cape.
This graphic novel by Art Spiegelman portrays the Holocaust with animal-headed characters.
This host turned a daytime talk show into a media empire and book club.
This chef and travel host wrote Kitchen Confidential.
This athletic brand's swoosh logo was designed by Carolyn Davidson.
This fast-food chain uses the slogan Have It Your Way.
This Italian dessert layers coffee-soaked ladyfingers with mascarpone.
This fermented Korean cabbage dish is commonly seasoned with chili and garlic.
This French fashion house is associated with the interlocking CC logo.
This company created the iPhone and Macintosh computer.
This database language uses SELECT, FROM, and WHERE clauses.
This 2020 global public health crisis was caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus.
This British computer scientist proposed the World Wide Web while at CERN.
